<?php.explanation: The opening tag that tells the PHP interpreter where PHP code starts. Every PHP script or block needs one.

echo.explanation: Outputs one or more strings to the page/console. It's a language construct, not a real function, so it doesn't need parentheses.

print.explanation: Outputs a single string, similar to echo, but always returns the value 1 so it can be used inside expressions.

if.explanation: Runs a block of code only when a condition is true.

elseif.explanation: Checks another condition when the previous if was false. Can also be written as two words: 'else if'.
elseif.example:
<?php
if ($score >= 90) {
    echo 'A';
} elseif ($score >= 80) {
    echo 'B';
} else {
    echo 'C';
}

else.explanation: Runs a block of code when the preceding if (or elseif) condition was false.

switch.explanation: Compares a value against several possible cases and runs the matching block.
switch.example:
<?php
switch ($day) {
    case 'Mon':
        echo 'Start of week';
        break;
    default:
        echo 'Another day';
}

case.explanation: Defines one branch to match against inside a switch statement.

for.explanation: A loop that repeats a block of code a set number of times, using a counter variable.

foreach.explanation: Loops over each element of an array, one at a time — the most common way to iterate arrays in PHP.

while.explanation: Repeats a block of code as long as a condition stays true, checked before each iteration.

do-while.explanation: Like a while loop, but the code runs once before the condition is checked, so it always executes at least once.

break.explanation: Immediately exits the nearest loop or switch statement.

continue.explanation: Skips the rest of the current loop iteration and moves on to the next one.

function.explanation: Defines a reusable block of code that can take inputs (parameters) and optionally return a value.

return.explanation: Exits a function and optionally sends a value back to whoever called it.

class.explanation: Defines a blueprint for creating objects that share properties and behavior.
class.example:
<?php
class Point {
    public int $x;
    public int $y;
}

interface.explanation: Describes a set of methods a class must implement, without providing the implementation itself.
interface.example:
<?php
interface Shape {
    public function area(): float;
}

extends.explanation: Makes one class inherit properties and methods from another (parent) class.
extends.example:
<?php
class Animal {
    public function speak() { echo '...'; }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    public function speak() { echo 'Woof!'; }
}

implements.explanation: Declares that a class provides everything required by one or more interfaces.
implements.example:
<?php
class Circle implements Shape {
    public function area(): float { return 3.14 * $this->r ** 2; }
}

public.explanation: Marks a property or method as accessible from anywhere, including outside the class.

private.explanation: Marks a property or method as only accessible from inside the class it's defined in.

protected.explanation: Marks a property or method as accessible from the class it's defined in and its subclasses, but not from outside.

static.explanation: Defines a property or method that belongs to the class itself, not to individual instances.
static.example:
<?php
class MathUtils {
    public static function square($x) { return $x * $x; }
}
MathUtils::square(4);

const.explanation: Defines a class-level (or global) constant whose value cannot change after it's declared.

new.explanation: Creates a new instance (object) from a class.

$this.explanation: Inside a method, refers to the current object instance the method is running on.
$this.example:
<?php
class Counter {
    private int $count = 0;
    public function increment() { $this->count++; }
}

parent.explanation: Refers to the parent class, most often used to call the parent's constructor or an overridden method.
parent.example:
<?php
class Dog extends Animal {
    public function __construct($name) {
        parent::__construct($name);
    }
}

try.explanation: Marks a block of code to attempt running, so that if it throws an error, you can catch and handle it instead of crashing.

catch.explanation: Runs when the matching try block throws an exception, giving you access to the exception object.

finally.explanation: Runs after the try/catch, whether or not an exception occurred — useful for cleanup.

throw.explanation: Raises an exception on purpose, stopping normal execution unless it's caught.
throw.example:
<?php
function check($age) {
    if ($age < 0) throw new InvalidArgumentException('Age cannot be negative');
}

namespace.explanation: Groups related classes/functions under a named container to avoid naming collisions between libraries.

use.explanation: Imports a class, function, or trait from another namespace so it can be referenced by its short name.

require.explanation: Includes and runs another PHP file, stopping the script with a fatal error if the file isn't found.

array.explanation: PHP's built-in data structure for storing an ordered list of values, or key-value pairs.

match.explanation: A more modern alternative to switch (PHP 8+) that compares strictly, returns a value directly, and requires no 'break'.

??.explanation: The null coalescing operator — returns the left value if it's set and not null, otherwise returns the right value.

abstract.explanation: Marks a class or method that can't be instantiated/called directly — it must be extended and implemented by a subclass.
abstract.example:
<?php
abstract class Shape {
    abstract public function area(): float;
}

final.explanation: Marks a class as unable to be extended, or a method as unable to be overridden by subclasses.

null.explanation: Represents the intentional absence of a value.
